question:
10. A cylindrical hole of radius \( r \) is bored through a cylinder of radius \( R \) ( \( r \leq R \) ) so that the axes intersect at right angles.
(i) Show that the area of the larger cylinder which is inside the smaller can be expressed in the form
\[
S=8 r^{2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1-v^{2}}{\sqrt{\left(1-v^{2}\right)\left(1-m^{2} v^{2}\right)}} d v \quad \text { where } \quad m=\frac{r}{R}
\]
(ii) If
\[
K=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d v}{\sqrt{\left(1-v^{2}\right)\left(1-m^{2} v^{2}\right)}} \text { and } E=\int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{\frac{1-m^{2} v^{2}}{1-v^{2}}} d v
\]
show that
\[
S=8\left[R^{2} E-\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right) K\right]
\]
solution:
Solution. Consider the sketch.

Let the two cylindrical surfaces be \( x^{2}+z^{2}=R^{2} \) and \( x^{2}+y^{2}=r^{2} \), where \( r \leq R \). The shaded area shown in the diagram is the part of the required area that lies in one octant. The equation of this surface is
\[
z=\sqrt{R^{2}-x^{2}}
\]

The required area is
\[
S=8 \iint \sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial y}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}\right)^{2}} d y d x
\]
where the double integral is over the region
\[
x^{2}+y^{2} \leq r^{2}, \quad x \geq 0, \quad y \geq 0 .
\]

Converted to an iterated integral, this becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
S & =8 \int_{0}^{r}\left(\int_{0}^{\sqrt{r^{2}-x^{2}}} \frac{R}{\sqrt{R^{2}-x^{2}}} d y\right) d x \\
& =8 R \int_{0}^{r} \sqrt{\frac{r^{2}-x^{2}}{R^{2}-x^{2}}} d x .
\end{aligned}
\]

Now let \( x / r=v \) and \( r / R=m \) and simplify further to get
\[
S=8 r^{2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1-v^{2}}{\sqrt{\left(1-v^{2}\right)\left(1-m^{2} v^{2}\right)}} d v,
\]
which completes part (i).
To obtain (ii), write \( r^{2}\left(1-v^{2}\right)=R^{2}\left(1-m^{2} v^{2}\right)-\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right) \) and substitute to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
S & =8 \int_{0}^{1} \frac{R^{2}\left(1-m^{2} v^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\left(1-v^{2}\right)\left(1-m^{2} v^{2}\right)}} d v-8 \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\left(1-v^{2}\right)\left(1-m^{2} v^{2}\right)}} d v \\
& =8\left[R^{2} E-\left(R^{2}-r^{2}\right) K\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. The integrals \( K \) and \( E \) are known as the complete elliptic integrals of the first and second kinds, respectively. Their values have been tabulated in terms of the parameter \( m \).

descriptive_long.question:
A cylindrical hole of radius \( smallradius \) is bored through a cylinder of radius \( largeradius \) ( \( smallradius \leq largeradius \) ) so that the axes intersect at right angles.
(i) Show that the area of the larger cylinder which is inside the smaller can be expressed in the form
\[
arearesult=8\,smallradius^{2}\int_{0}^{1}\frac{1-scaleratio^{2}}{\sqrt{\left(1-scaleratio^{2}\right)\left(1-ratparameter^{2} scaleratio^{2}\right)}}\,d scaleratio\quad\text{ where }\quad ratparameter=\frac{smallradius}{largeradius}
\]
(ii) If
\[
elliptickone=\int_{0}^{1}\frac{d scaleratio}{\sqrt{\left(1-scaleratio^{2}\right)\left(1-ratparameter^{2} scaleratio^{2}\right)}}\quad\text{and}\quad elliptictwo=\int_{0}^{1}\sqrt{\frac{1-ratparameter^{2} scaleratio^{2}}{1-scaleratio^{2}}}\,d scaleratio
\]
show that
\[
arearesult=8\left[largeradius^{2}\,elliptictwo-\left(largeradius^{2}-smallradius^{2}\right)\,elliptickone\right]
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Consider the sketch.

Let the two cylindrical surfaces be \( axisfirst^{2}+axisthird^{2}=largeradius^{2} \) and \( axisfirst^{2}+axissecond^{2}=smallradius^{2} \), where \( smallradius \leq largeradius \). The shaded area shown in the diagram is the part of the required area that lies in one octant. The equation of this surface is
\[
axisthird=\sqrt{largeradius^{2}-axisfirst^{2}}
\]

The required area is
\[
arearesult=8\iint\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\partial axisthird}{\partial axissecond}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\partial axisthird}{\partial axisfirst}\right)^{2}}\,d axissecond\,d axisfirst
\]
where the double integral is over the region
\[
axisfirst^{2}+axissecond^{2}\leq smallradius^{2},\quad axisfirst\geq0,\quad axissecond\geq0.
\]

Converted to an iterated integral, this becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
arearesult&=8\int_{0}^{smallradius}\left(\int_{0}^{\sqrt{smallradius^{2}-axisfirst^{2}}}\frac{largeradius}{\sqrt{largeradius^{2}-axisfirst^{2}}}\,d axissecond\right)d axisfirst\\
&=8\,largeradius\int_{0}^{smallradius}\sqrt{\frac{smallradius^{2}-axisfirst^{2}}{largeradius^{2}-axisfirst^{2}}}\,d axisfirst.
\end{aligned}
\]

Now let \( axisfirst/smallradius=scaleratio \) and \( smallradius/largeradius=ratparameter \) and simplify further to get
\[
arearesult=8\,smallradius^{2}\int_{0}^{1}\frac{1-scaleratio^{2}}{\sqrt{(1-scaleratio^{2})(1-ratparameter^{2} scaleratio^{2})}}\,d scaleratio,
\]
which completes part (i).

To obtain (ii), write \( smallradius^{2}(1-scaleratio^{2})=largeradius^{2}(1-ratparameter^{2} scaleratio^{2})-(largeradius^{2}-smallradius^{2}) \) and substitute to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
arearesult&=8\int_{0}^{1}\frac{largeradius^{2}(1-ratparameter^{2} scaleratio^{2})}{\sqrt{(1-scaleratio^{2})(1-ratparameter^{2} scaleratio^{2})}}\,d scaleratio-8\int_{0}^{1}\frac{(largeradius^{2}-smallradius^{2})}{\sqrt{(1-scaleratio^{2})(1-ratparameter^{2} scaleratio^{2})}}\,d scaleratio\\
&=8\left[largeradius^{2}\,elliptictwo-(largeradius^{2}-smallradius^{2})\,elliptickone\right].
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. The integrals \( elliptickone \) and \( elliptictwo \) are known as the complete elliptic integrals of the first and second kinds, respectively. Their values have been tabulated in terms of the parameter \( ratparameter \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
10. A cylindrical hole of radius \( lanternarc \) is bored through a cylinder of radius \( starlingdew \) ( \( lanternarc \leq starlingdew \) ) so that the axes intersect at right angles.
(i) Show that the area of the larger cylinder which is inside the smaller can be expressed in the form
\[
panoramal=8 \, lanternarc^{2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1-cobblestone^{2}}{\sqrt{\left(1-cobblestone^{2}\right)\left(1-quartzbeam^{2} \, cobblestone^{2}\right)}} d cobblestone \quad \text { where } \quad quartzbeam=\frac{lanternarc}{starlingdew}
\]
(ii) If
\[
willowcrest=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d cobblestone}{\sqrt{\left(1-cobblestone^{2}\right)\left(1-quartzbeam^{2} \, cobblestone^{2}\right)}} \text { and } emberthroat=\int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{\frac{1-quartzbeam^{2} \, cobblestone^{2}}{1-cobblestone^{2}}} d cobblestone
\]
show that
\[
panoramal=8\left[starlingdew^{2} \, emberthroat-\left(starlingdew^{2}-lanternarc^{2}\right) \, willowcrest\right]
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Consider the sketch.

Let the two cylindrical surfaces be \( meadowway^{2}+glyphtrack^{2}=starlingdew^{2} \) and \( meadowway^{2}+harborline^{2}=lanternarc^{2} \), where \( lanternarc \leq starlingdew \). The shaded area shown in the diagram is the part of the required area that lies in one octant. The equation of this surface is
\[
glyphtrack=\sqrt{starlingdew^{2}-meadowway^{2}}
\]

The required area is
\[
panoramal=8 \iint \sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\partial glyphtrack}{\partial harborline}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\partial glyphtrack}{\partial meadowway}\right)^{2}} d harborline \, d meadowway
\]
where the double integral is over the region
\[
meadowway^{2}+harborline^{2} \leq lanternarc^{2}, \quad meadowway \geq 0, \quad harborline \geq 0 .
\]

Converted to an iterated integral, this becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
panoramal & =8 \int_{0}^{lanternarc}\left(\int_{0}^{\sqrt{lanternarc^{2}-meadowway^{2}}} \frac{starlingdew}{\sqrt{starlingdew^{2}-meadowway^{2}}} d harborline\right) d meadowway \\
& =8 \, starlingdew \int_{0}^{lanternarc} \sqrt{\frac{lanternarc^{2}-meadowway^{2}}{starlingdew^{2}-meadowway^{2}}} d meadowway .
\end{aligned}
\]

Now let \( meadowway / lanternarc=cobblestone \) and \( lanternarc / starlingdew=quartzbeam \) and simplify further to get
\[
panoramal=8 \, lanternarc^{2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1-cobblestone^{2}}{\sqrt{\left(1-cobblestone^{2}\right)\left(1-quartzbeam^{2} \, cobblestone^{2}\right)}} d cobblestone,
\]
which completes part (i).
To obtain (ii), write \( lanternarc^{2}\left(1-cobblestone^{2}\right)=starlingdew^{2}\left(1-quartzbeam^{2} \, cobblestone^{2}\right)-\left(starlingdew^{2}-lanternarc^{2}\right) \) and substitute to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
panoramal & =8 \int_{0}^{1} \frac{starlingdew^{2}\left(1-quartzbeam^{2} \, cobblestone^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\left(1-cobblestone^{2}\right)\left(1-quartzbeam^{2} \, cobblestone^{2}\right)}} d cobblestone-8 \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\left(starlingdew^{2}-lanternarc^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\left(1-cobblestone^{2}\right)\left(1-quartzbeam^{2} \, cobblestone^{2}\right)}} d cobblestone \\
& =8\left[starlingdew^{2} \, emberthroat-\left(starlingdew^{2}-lanternarc^{2}\right) \, willowcrest\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. The integrals \( willowcrest \) and \( emberthroat \) are known as the complete elliptic integrals of the first and second kinds, respectively. Their values have been tabulated in terms of the parameter \( quartzbeam \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
10. A cylindrical hole of radius \( diameter \) is bored through a cylinder of radius \( circumference \) ( \( diameter \leq circumference \) ) so that the axes intersect at right angles.
(i) Show that the area of the larger cylinder which is inside the smaller can be expressed in the form
\[
perimeter=8 \, diameter^{2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1-constantval^{2}}{\sqrt{\left(1-constantval^{2}\right)\left(1-productval^{2} \, constantval^{2}\right)}} d constantval \quad \text { where } \quad productval=\frac{diameter}{circumference}
\]
(ii) If
\[
incomplete=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d constantval}{\sqrt{\left(1-constantval^{2}\right)\left(1-productval^{2} \, constantval^{2}\right)}} \text { and } hyperbolic=\int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{\frac{1-productval^{2} \, constantval^{2}}{1-constantval^{2}}} d constantval
\]
show that
\[
perimeter=8\left[circumference^{2} \, hyperbolic-\left(circumference^{2}-diameter^{2}\right) \, incomplete\right]
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Consider the sketch.

Let the two cylindrical surfaces be \( verticalcoord^{2}+deepcoord^{2}=circumference^{2} \) and \( verticalcoord^{2}+horizontalcoord^{2}=diameter^{2} \), where \( diameter \leq circumference \). The shaded area shown in the diagram is the part of the required area that lies in one octant. The equation of this surface is
\[
deepcoord=\sqrt{circumference^{2}-verticalcoord^{2}}
\]

The required area is
\[
perimeter=8 \iint \sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\partial deepcoord}{\partial horizontalcoord}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\partial deepcoord}{\partial verticalcoord}\right)^{2}} d horizontalcoord d verticalcoord
\]
where the double integral is over the region
\[
verticalcoord^{2}+horizontalcoord^{2} \leq diameter^{2}, \quad verticalcoord \geq 0, \quad horizontalcoord \geq 0 .
\]

Converted to an iterated integral, this becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
perimeter & =8 \int_{0}^{diameter}\left(\int_{0}^{\sqrt{diameter^{2}-verticalcoord^{2}}} \frac{circumference}{\sqrt{circumference^{2}-verticalcoord^{2}}} d horizontalcoord\right) d verticalcoord \\
& =8 \, circumference \int_{0}^{diameter} \sqrt{\frac{diameter^{2}-verticalcoord^{2}}{circumference^{2}-verticalcoord^{2}}} d verticalcoord .
\end{aligned}
\]

Now let \( verticalcoord / diameter=constantval \) and \( diameter / circumference=productval \) and simplify further to get
\[
perimeter=8 \, diameter^{2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1-constantval^{2}}{\sqrt{\left(1-constantval^{2}\right)\left(1-productval^{2} \, constantval^{2}\right)}} d constantval,
\]
which completes part (i).
To obtain (ii), write \( diameter^{2}\left(1-constantval^{2}\right)=circumference^{2}\left(1-productval^{2} \, constantval^{2}\right)-\left(circumference^{2}-diameter^{2}\right) \) and substitute to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
perimeter & =8 \int_{0}^{1} \frac{circumference^{2}\left(1-productval^{2} \, constantval^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\left(1-constantval^{2}\right)\left(1-productval^{2} \, constantval^{2}\right)}} d constantval-8 \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\left(circumference^{2}-diameter^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\left(1-constantval^{2}\right)\left(1-productval^{2} \, constantval^{2}\right)}} d constantval \\
& =8\left[circumference^{2} \, hyperbolic-\left(circumference^{2}-diameter^{2}\right) \, incomplete\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. The integrals \( incomplete \) and \( hyperbolic \) are known as the complete elliptic integrals of the first and second kinds, respectively. Their values have been tabulated in terms of the parameter \( productval \).

garbled_string.question:
10. A cylindrical hole of radius \( nxpdcove \) is bored through a cylinder of radius \( uwbrhaes \) ( \( nxpdcove \leq uwbrhaes \) ) so that the axes intersect at right angles.
(i) Show that the area of the larger cylinder which is inside the smaller can be expressed in the form
\[
qzxwvtnp=8 nxpdcove^{2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1-dsxjplzi^{2}}{\sqrt{\left(1-dsxjplzi^{2}\right)\left(1-kztqpluw^{2} dsxjplzi^{2}\right)}} d dsxjplzi \quad \text { where } \quad kztqpluw=\frac{nxpdcove}{uwbrhaes}
\]
(ii) If
\[
oblghrse=\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d dsxjplzi}{\sqrt{\left(1-dsxjplzi^{2}\right)\left(1-kztqpluw^{2} dsxjplzi^{2}\right)}} \text { and } fsqvlimk=\int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{\frac{1-kztqpluw^{2} dsxjplzi^{2}}{1-dsxjplzi^{2}}} d dsxjplzi
\]
show that
\[
qzxwvtnp=8\left[uwbrhaes^{2} fsqvlimk-\left(uwbrhaes^{2}-nxpdcove^{2}\right) oblghrse\right]
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Consider the sketch.

Let the two cylindrical surfaces be \( hjgrksla^{2}+lmfqcrua^{2}=uwbrhaes^{2} \) and \( hjgrksla^{2}+pbvndtqe^{2}=nxpdcove^{2} \), where \( nxpdcove \leq uwbrhaes \). The shaded area shown in the diagram is the part of the required area that lies in one octant. The equation of this surface is
\[
lmfqcrua=\sqrt{uwbrhaes^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}}
\]

The required area is
\[
qzxwvtnp=8 \iint \sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\partial lmfqcrua}{\partial pbvndtqe}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{\partial lmfqcrua}{\partial hjgrksla}\right)^{2}} d pbvndtqe d hjgrksla
\]
where the double integral is over the region
\[
hjgrksla^{2}+pbvndtqe^{2} \leq nxpdcove^{2}, \quad hjgrksla \geq 0, \quad pbvndtqe \geq 0 .
\]

Converted to an iterated integral, this becomes
\[
\begin{aligned}
qzxwvtnp & =8 \int_{0}^{nxpdcove}\left(\int_{0}^{\sqrt{nxpdcove^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}}} \frac{uwbrhaes}{\sqrt{uwbrhaes^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}}} d pbvndtqe\right) d hjgrksla \\
& =8 uwbrhaes \int_{0}^{nxpdcove} \sqrt{\frac{nxpdcove^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}}{uwbrhaes^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}}} d hjgrksla .
\end{aligned}
\]

Now let \( hjgrksla / nxpdcove=dsxjplzi \) and \( nxpdcove / uwbrhaes=kztqpluw \) and simplify further to get
\[
qzxwvtnp=8 nxpdcove^{2} \int_{0}^{1} \frac{1-dsxjplzi^{2}}{\sqrt{\left(1-dsxjplzi^{2}\right)\left(1-kztqpluw^{2} dsxjplzi^{2}\right)}} d dsxjplzi,
\]
which completes part (i).
To obtain (ii), write \( nxpdcove^{2}\left(1-dsxjplzi^{2}\right)=uwbrhaes^{2}\left(1-kztqpluw^{2} dsxjplzi^{2}\right)-\left(uwbrhaes^{2}-nxpdcove^{2}\right) \) and substitute to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
qzxwvtnp & =8 \int_{0}^{1} \frac{uwbrhaes^{2}\left(1-kztqpluw^{2} dsxjplzi^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\left(1-dsxjplzi^{2}\right)\left(1-kztqpluw^{2} dsxjplzi^{2}\right)}} d dsxjplzi-8 \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\left(uwbrhaes^{2}-nxpdcove^{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\left(1-dsxjplzi^{2}\right)\left(1-kztqpluw^{2} dsxjplzi^{2}\right)}} d dsxjplzi \\
& =8\left[uwbrhaes^{2} fsqvlimk-\left(uwbrhaes^{2}-nxpdcove^{2}\right) oblghrse\right]
\end{aligned}
\]

Remark. The integrals \( oblghrse \) and \( fsqvlimk \) are known as the complete elliptic integrals of the first and second kinds, respectively. Their values have been tabulated in terms of the parameter \( kztqpluw \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let 0<r\leq R and let the two axes form an acute angle 0<\varphi <\pi /2.

Large (host) cylinder  
 C_R : y^2+z^2 = R^2   (axis Ox)

Obliquely-drilled cylinder  
 C_{r,\varphi } : y^2 + (z cos \varphi  - x sin \varphi )^2 = r^2,

whose axis  
 \ell  : t \mapsto  (t cos \varphi , 0, t sin \varphi )

lies in the xz-plane and meets the x-axis at the angle \varphi .

Denote by S(\varphi ) the total area of that part of the lateral surface of C_R which is contained in the solid C_{r,\varphi }.  (No half-space restriction - both z\geq 0 and z\leq 0 are included.)

Introduce the dimensionless ratio n=r/R\in (0,1].

(a)  Prove the representation  
  S(\varphi )= 8 r^2 csc \varphi  \int _0^1 (1-w^2)/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)} dw.    (1)

(b)  Using the complete elliptic integrals  
  K(n)=\int _0^1 dw/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)},   
  E(n)=\int _0^1 \sqrt{(1-n^2w^2)/(1-w^2)} dw,  

 show the closed form  
  S(\varphi )= 8 csc \varphi  [ R^2E(n) - (R^2-r^2)K(n) ].           (2)

(c)  Verify the limiting values  
  lim_{\varphi \to 0^+} S(\varphi )=+\infty  ,  lim_{\varphi \to \pi /2} S(\varphi )=8[ R^2E(n) - (R^2-r^2)K(n) ] ,

and explain geometrically why the second limit agrees with the classical right-angle drilling result.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout put  
 n=r/R  (0<n\leq 1),  \sigma =sin \varphi >0, \tau =cos \varphi >0.

Step 1 - Parameterising the host cylinder.  
With the standard angular parameter \theta \in [0,2\pi )

 X(x,\theta )=(x, R cos \theta , R sin \theta )                                    (1)

every fixed \theta  traces a generator parallel to the x-axis.  
Because  
 |\partial X/\partial x \times  \partial X/\partial \theta | = R,  
the surface element is

 dS = R dx d\theta .                                                  (2)

Step 2 - The x-interval cut out by the bore.  
Insert (1) into the inequality describing the interior of C_{r,\varphi }:

 R^2 cos^2\theta  + (R sin \theta  \tau  - x \sigma )^2 \leq  r^2.                           (3)

For a fixed \theta  put

 \Delta (\theta )=\sqrt{r^2 - R^2 cos^2\theta },  defined iff |cos \theta |\leq n.              (4)

When |cos \theta |>n the inequality has no solution; otherwise (3) becomes

 | x - (R \tau /\sigma ) sin \theta  | \leq  \Delta (\theta )/\sigma .                                (5)

Hence, for every \theta  with |cos \theta |\leq n the admissible x-values form the interval

 I_\theta  = [ (R \tau  sin \theta  - \Delta )/\sigma  , (R \tau  sin \theta  + \Delta )/\sigma  ],              (6)

whose length is

 L(\theta )= 2\Delta (\theta )/\sigma  = 2R \sigma ^{-1}\sqrt{n^2 - cos^2\theta }.                       (7)

Step 3 - First area integral (full cylinder, not only z\geq 0).  
The set {|cos \theta |\leq n} consists of the two symmetric arcs  
 [\theta _0, \pi -\theta _0] \cup  [\pi +\theta _0, 2\pi -\theta _0], \theta _0 := arccos n \in  (0,\pi /2].  
Using (2) and (7),

 S(\varphi )= R\int _{|cos \theta |\leq n} L(\theta ) d\theta 
     = 2R\int _{\theta _0}^{\pi -\theta _0} L(\theta ) d\theta   (symmetry \theta \mapsto \theta +\pi )
     = 4R^2 \sigma ^{-1}\int _{\theta _0}^{\pi -\theta _0} \sqrt{n^2 - cos^2\theta } d\theta .                (8)

Step 4 - Reduction to a standard integral.  
Put cos \theta  = n w (so w\in [-1,1]).  Because

 d\theta  = -n dw /\sqrt{1-n^2w^2} ,

and the integrand is an even function of w, (8) becomes

 S(\varphi )= 8R^2n^2 \sigma ^{-1} \int _0^1 \sqrt{1-w^2}/\sqrt{1-n^2w^2} dw.                 (9)

Write \sqrt{1-w^2} = (1-w^2)/\sqrt{1-w^2} to get

 S(\varphi )= 8R^2n^2 \sigma ^{-1} \int _0^1 (1-w^2)/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)} dw.        (10)

Since r^2=n^2R^2 and csc \varphi =\sigma ^{-1}, (10) is exactly formula (1).

Step 5 - Expressing the integral through K and E.  
Define  

 I_1(n)=\int _0^1 (1-w^2)/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)} dw.                     (11)

Write 1-w^2 = 1-n^2w^2 + (n^2-1)w^2 and split I_1:

 I_1(n)= \int _0^1 \sqrt{(1-n^2w^2)/(1-w^2)} dw
        -(1-n^2)\int _0^1 w^2/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)} dw.                  (12)

The first integral is E(n).  
The classical identity

 K(n)-E(n)= n^2\int _0^1 w^2/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)} dw                  (13)

implies  

 \int  w^2/\sqrt{...} dw = [ K(n)-E(n) ] / n^2.

Substituting in (12) gives

 I_1(n)= [ E(n) - (1-n^2)K(n) ] / n^2.                          (14)

Step 6 - Closed form for S(\varphi ).  
Insert (14) in (1):

 S(\varphi )= 8r^2 csc \varphi  \cdot  I_1(n)
      = 8n^2R^2 csc \varphi  \cdot  [E(n) - (1-n^2)K(n)]/n^2
      = 8 csc \varphi  [ R^2E(n) - (R^2-r^2)K(n) ],                     (15)

which is formula (2).

Step 7 - The requested limits.  

(i)  As \varphi \to 0^+, sin \varphi \approx \varphi , hence S(\varphi )\sim (8/\varphi )[R^2E(n) - (R^2-r^2)K(n)] \to  +\infty .  
  Geometrically, when the two axes nearly coincide the small bore runs
  almost tangentially to the host surface, producing an arbitrarily
  long (and therefore unbounded) strip of intersection.

(ii)  For \varphi \to \pi /2 we have sin \varphi \to 1, csc \varphi \to 1, and (15) yields

  lim_{\varphi \to \pi /2} S(\varphi ) = 8[ R^2E(n) - (R^2-r^2)K(n) ].              (16)

  This matches the classical right-angle drilling problem: when \varphi =\pi /2
  the two axes are perpendicular, the solid C_{r,\varphi } possesses the
  mirror symmetry z\mapsto -z, and the complete intersection with the entire
  lateral surface of C_R is exactly twice the area that appears when
  only the half-space z\geq 0 is considered in elementary treatments.

Thus parts (a)-(c) are proved.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Additional geometric parameter: the cylinders now intersect at an arbitrary angle φ (0<φ<π/2); the orthogonal case is only a limit. All formulas therefore depend simultaneously on n=r/R and on φ, making the geometry genuinely 2-parametric.

2. Coordinate‐free setup: solving the problem required an orthogonal change of variables (u,v,y) adapted to the skew axis ℓ, not merely the standard Cartesian projection used in the original problem.

3. Constraint by a half-space: retaining only H₊ complicates the bounds, forcing a careful treatment of the allowed θ-interval and doubling issues.

4. Integral manipulation: the raw integral that appears is ∫√(1−w²)/√(1−n²w²), not one of the tabulated elliptic forms. Turning it into a combination of E(n) and K(n) needs the non-trivial identity K−E = n²∫w²/…, a step absent from the original exercise.

5. Asymptotic analysis: part (c) asks for limiting behaviour as φ approaches 0 or π/2, introducing an extra layer of calculus and geometric interpretation.

Altogether the problem demands mastery of geometry in oblique coordinates, advanced integral reduction techniques, classical relations between elliptic integrals, and asymptotic reasoning—substantially more technical and conceptual effort than the right-angle, single-parameter case.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let 0<r\leq R and let the two axes form an acute angle 0<\varphi <\pi /2.

Large (host) cylinder  
 C_R : y^2+z^2 = R^2   (axis Ox)

Obliquely-drilled cylinder  
 C_{r,\varphi } : y^2 + (z cos \varphi  - x sin \varphi )^2 = r^2,

whose axis  
 \ell  : t \mapsto  (t cos \varphi , 0, t sin \varphi )

lies in the xz-plane and meets the x-axis at the angle \varphi .

Denote by S(\varphi ) the total area of that part of the lateral surface of C_R which is contained in the solid C_{r,\varphi }.  (No half-space restriction - both z\geq 0 and z\leq 0 are included.)

Introduce the dimensionless ratio n=r/R\in (0,1].

(a)  Prove the representation  
  S(\varphi )= 8 r^2 csc \varphi  \int _0^1 (1-w^2)/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)} dw.    (1)

(b)  Using the complete elliptic integrals  
  K(n)=\int _0^1 dw/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)},   
  E(n)=\int _0^1 \sqrt{(1-n^2w^2)/(1-w^2)} dw,  

 show the closed form  
  S(\varphi )= 8 csc \varphi  [ R^2E(n) - (R^2-r^2)K(n) ].           (2)

(c)  Verify the limiting values  
  lim_{\varphi \to 0^+} S(\varphi )=+\infty  ,  lim_{\varphi \to \pi /2} S(\varphi )=8[ R^2E(n) - (R^2-r^2)K(n) ] ,

and explain geometrically why the second limit agrees with the classical right-angle drilling result.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout put  
 n=r/R  (0<n\leq 1),  \sigma =sin \varphi >0, \tau =cos \varphi >0.

Step 1 - Parameterising the host cylinder.  
With the standard angular parameter \theta \in [0,2\pi )

 X(x,\theta )=(x, R cos \theta , R sin \theta )                                    (1)

every fixed \theta  traces a generator parallel to the x-axis.  
Because  
 |\partial X/\partial x \times  \partial X/\partial \theta | = R,  
the surface element is

 dS = R dx d\theta .                                                  (2)

Step 2 - The x-interval cut out by the bore.  
Insert (1) into the inequality describing the interior of C_{r,\varphi }:

 R^2 cos^2\theta  + (R sin \theta  \tau  - x \sigma )^2 \leq  r^2.                           (3)

For a fixed \theta  put

 \Delta (\theta )=\sqrt{r^2 - R^2 cos^2\theta },  defined iff |cos \theta |\leq n.              (4)

When |cos \theta |>n the inequality has no solution; otherwise (3) becomes

 | x - (R \tau /\sigma ) sin \theta  | \leq  \Delta (\theta )/\sigma .                                (5)

Hence, for every \theta  with |cos \theta |\leq n the admissible x-values form the interval

 I_\theta  = [ (R \tau  sin \theta  - \Delta )/\sigma  , (R \tau  sin \theta  + \Delta )/\sigma  ],              (6)

whose length is

 L(\theta )= 2\Delta (\theta )/\sigma  = 2R \sigma ^{-1}\sqrt{n^2 - cos^2\theta }.                       (7)

Step 3 - First area integral (full cylinder, not only z\geq 0).  
The set {|cos \theta |\leq n} consists of the two symmetric arcs  
 [\theta _0, \pi -\theta _0] \cup  [\pi +\theta _0, 2\pi -\theta _0], \theta _0 := arccos n \in  (0,\pi /2].  
Using (2) and (7),

 S(\varphi )= R\int _{|cos \theta |\leq n} L(\theta ) d\theta 
     = 2R\int _{\theta _0}^{\pi -\theta _0} L(\theta ) d\theta   (symmetry \theta \mapsto \theta +\pi )
     = 4R^2 \sigma ^{-1}\int _{\theta _0}^{\pi -\theta _0} \sqrt{n^2 - cos^2\theta } d\theta .                (8)

Step 4 - Reduction to a standard integral.  
Put cos \theta  = n w (so w\in [-1,1]).  Because

 d\theta  = -n dw /\sqrt{1-n^2w^2} ,

and the integrand is an even function of w, (8) becomes

 S(\varphi )= 8R^2n^2 \sigma ^{-1} \int _0^1 \sqrt{1-w^2}/\sqrt{1-n^2w^2} dw.                 (9)

Write \sqrt{1-w^2} = (1-w^2)/\sqrt{1-w^2} to get

 S(\varphi )= 8R^2n^2 \sigma ^{-1} \int _0^1 (1-w^2)/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)} dw.        (10)

Since r^2=n^2R^2 and csc \varphi =\sigma ^{-1}, (10) is exactly formula (1).

Step 5 - Expressing the integral through K and E.  
Define  

 I_1(n)=\int _0^1 (1-w^2)/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)} dw.                     (11)

Write 1-w^2 = 1-n^2w^2 + (n^2-1)w^2 and split I_1:

 I_1(n)= \int _0^1 \sqrt{(1-n^2w^2)/(1-w^2)} dw
        -(1-n^2)\int _0^1 w^2/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)} dw.                  (12)

The first integral is E(n).  
The classical identity

 K(n)-E(n)= n^2\int _0^1 w^2/\sqrt{(1-w^2)(1-n^2w^2)} dw                  (13)

implies  

 \int  w^2/\sqrt{...} dw = [ K(n)-E(n) ] / n^2.

Substituting in (12) gives

 I_1(n)= [ E(n) - (1-n^2)K(n) ] / n^2.                          (14)

Step 6 - Closed form for S(\varphi ).  
Insert (14) in (1):

 S(\varphi )= 8r^2 csc \varphi  \cdot  I_1(n)
      = 8n^2R^2 csc \varphi  \cdot  [E(n) - (1-n^2)K(n)]/n^2
      = 8 csc \varphi  [ R^2E(n) - (R^2-r^2)K(n) ],                     (15)

which is formula (2).

Step 7 - The requested limits.  

(i)  As \varphi \to 0^+, sin \varphi \approx \varphi , hence S(\varphi )\sim (8/\varphi )[R^2E(n) - (R^2-r^2)K(n)] \to  +\infty .  
  Geometrically, when the two axes nearly coincide the small bore runs
  almost tangentially to the host surface, producing an arbitrarily
  long (and therefore unbounded) strip of intersection.

(ii)  For \varphi \to \pi /2 we have sin \varphi \to 1, csc \varphi \to 1, and (15) yields

  lim_{\varphi \to \pi /2} S(\varphi ) = 8[ R^2E(n) - (R^2-r^2)K(n) ].              (16)

  This matches the classical right-angle drilling problem: when \varphi =\pi /2
  the two axes are perpendicular, the solid C_{r,\varphi } possesses the
  mirror symmetry z\mapsto -z, and the complete intersection with the entire
  lateral surface of C_R is exactly twice the area that appears when
  only the half-space z\geq 0 is considered in elementary treatments.

Thus parts (a)-(c) are proved.

2. Coordinate‐free setup: solving the problem required an orthogonal change of variables (u,v,y) adapted to the skew axis ℓ, not merely the standard Cartesian projection used in the original problem.

3. Constraint by a half-space: retaining only H₊ complicates the bounds, forcing a careful treatment of the allowed θ-interval and doubling issues.

4. Integral manipulation: the raw integral that appears is ∫√(1−w²)/√(1−n²w²), not one of the tabulated elliptic forms. Turning it into a combination of E(n) and K(n) needs the non-trivial identity K−E = n²∫w²/…, a step absent from the original exercise.
